sections.title: Inversions with negative adverbial words and phrases

content.subtitle: Form
items: negative adverbial word/phrase + question form

content.subtitle: Example
items: No sooner had I accepted the job than they told me I had to work weekends.

content.subtitle: Common negative adverbials
items: Never: Never have I worked so hard in all my life.
Rarely: Rarely have I worked so hard in all my life.
Seldom: Seldom have I worked so hard in all my life.
No sooner (…than): No sooner had Matt started work than he resigned.
Hardly (…when): Hardly had Matt started work when he resigned.
Not only (…but also/too): Not only were you late for work, but you had also forgotten the report.
Under no circumstances: Under no circumstances are employees allowed to leave the building without permission.
At no time/point: At no time/point was I told what the job involved.
Little: Little did I realise that I would become managing director just two years later.
Not until: Not until the next day did I hear that I had got the job.
Only: Only at the end of the interview did I think I had a chance of getting the job.

content.subtitle: Note on 'little'
content.description: Little is used with verbs of thought (realise, know, suspect, etc) and means 'I did not realise/etc at all'.
content.example: Little did I know how things were going to turn out. (= I did not know at all how things were going to turn out.)

content.subtitle: Note on 'not until' and 'only'
content.description: Be careful about which verb to invert.
examples: ✓ Not until I had finished my homework was I allowed to go out.
✗ Not until had I finished my homework was I allowed to go out.
✓ Only when I had finished my homework was I allowed to go out.
✗ Only when had I finished my homework was I allowed to go out.

content.subtitle: Short answers with so, neither, nor
examples: 'My sister doesn't like getting ready for work, and neither do I / nor do I.'

content.subtitle: After as, so and such
examples: The manager was nervous about the director's visit, as were the rest of the staff.
So late was it that there was no one in the office.
Such a hot day was it that no one wanted to work.

content.subtitle: In conditional sentences
examples: Were our staff better trained, we might make a larger profit. (= If our staff were better trained ...)
Had I known about the vacancy, I would have applied. (= If I had known ...)

content.subtitle: Note
content.description: Remember that 'So am/do/have I' is used to agree with a positive statement and 'Neither/Nor am/do/have I' is used to agree with a negative statement.
examples: I really like my job. 'So do I.'
I really don't like my job. 'Neither do I / Nor do I.'

content.subtitle: Use
items: We use 's with singular nouns, including names, and with irregular plurals which do not end in -s: That's the manager's car, over there. / I think that women's rights should be protected by law.
We just add an apostrophe to regular plural nouns ending in -s: The workers' pay was increased by ten percent.
We use 's or s' in some time expressions: After an hour's wait, I finally got to see the manager. / I'll be at the factory in about ten minutes' time.

We usually use 's or s' with people (or groups composed of people) and animals. For other things, we normally use 'of the/my/etc': Is this John's briefcase? / The vet had a close look at the cat's paw. / The technician had a close look at the back of my computer. (not ...at my computer's back)

content.subtitle: Notes
items: With singular names ending in -s, some people use 's and some people just add an apostrophe. Both are correct, although using 's is more common: The report is on Charles's computer in the office. / The report is on Charles' computer in the office.
With plural names ending in -s, we just add an apostrophe: The Smiths' business eventually closed down.

With phrases, the possessive 's and s' must go at the end of the whole phrase: Tom, Dick and Harry's office is around here somewhere. / The President of France's visit to England will be good for business.

content.subtitle: Possessive determiners
content.description: my, your, his, her, its, our, their (come before a noun)
content.example: I'm really excited about my new job. / Are you looking forward to meeting your new boss?

content.subtitle: Possessive pronouns
content.description: mine, yours, his, hers, ours, yours, theirs (used instead of a noun)
examples: My new job is great. How about yours? (= ...your job)
Our business is doing quite well, but I hear Simon and Julie are having problems with theirs. (= ...their business)
Possessive pronouns can also be used after 'of' to show possession: She's a colleague of mine. (= ...one of my colleagues) / Are they customers of yours?

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: A common mistake is using a possessive pronoun instead of a possessive determiner: ✓ I don't really get on so well with my boss. ✗ I don't really get on so well with mine boss.
Using a determiner instead of a pronoun: ✓ I know your job is dangerous, but did Frank and Jenny tell you about theirs? ✗ I know your job is dangerous, but did Frank and Jenny tell you about their?
There are no apostrophes in possessive pronouns: ✗ I know your job is dangerous, but did Frank and Jenny tell you about their's?

Don't get confused between 'its' (possessive determiner, without an apostrophe) and 'it's' (contraction for it is or it has, with an apostrophe): Our company is hoping to increase its share of the market.

items.sentence: Hardly had we started the fire alarm went.
No sooner had we set off my brother needed the toilet.
No sooner were the animals caught they escaped again.
Hardly had I closed my eyes I dropped off to sleep.
No sooner do I do the washing there's another pile to be done.
Hardly were we in position the bomb went off.
No sooner ______ down than the phone rang.
Never ______ a greater day in the history of mankind.
At no time ______ that he was the killer.
Little ______ that I knew his secret.
Not until ______ her did I realise how beautiful she was.
Not only ______ my keys, I've lost my wallet, too.
Rarely ______ about the environment enough to give up their car.
Only when ______ may vehicles cross the train tracks.
options: A is the green light on
B the green light is on
C did the green light be on
D on is the green light

items.sentence: Seldom ______ such a rude person!
Only after doing his chores ______ to go to meet his friends.

sections.title: C: Error correction (Inversions)
sections.instructions: If a word or phrase in bold is correct, put a tick (✓). If it is incorrect, rewrite it correctly.

items.sentence: Hardly Tom had opened the door when the dog ran out into the street.
Only when I read the book did I realise why it was so special.
Not only you passed the exam, but you also got an A!
Not until do I see Greg next week will I know whether he got the job.
No sooner had we found our seats than the film began.
At no point while the race is taking place members of the public are not allowed onto the track.
items.answer: are members of the public allowed

items.sentence: Under no circumstances do the equipment to be used without supervision.
items.answer: is the equipment to be used

items.sentence: Little I thought that one day my best friend would become prime minister.
Never did I be so pleased to see someone else succeed.
Hardly I had put the baby to bed when she started crying.

sections.title: D: Complete using inversions
sections.instructions: Complete using the correct form of the words in brackets.

items.sentence: No sooner (1) (I / start) working at Taylforth Nielsen, the computer company, than I realised it was the perfect opportunity for my hobby: hacking!
Don't get me wrong. At no point (2) (I / have) ever stolen anything.
Not only (3) (I / like) proving I can do it, but sometimes (4) (it / help) a company to build a better security system.
Rarely (5) (they / see) it like that, of course.
I decided I would see if I could break into the Grand Bank system. Little (6) (I / realise) what was waiting for me.
The second day of working there, hardly (7) (everyone else / leave) when I started to try simple passwords.
Seldom (8) (this / is) enough, though. You usually have to work much harder than that.
Not until midnight (9) (I / get) access to the Grand Bank system.
Hardly (10) (I / have) time to look around the system when the door to my office flew open and three men rushed in.
Only when (11) (they / show) me their ID (12) (I / realise) that it was the police.
They had been expecting something like this and had traced my computer! Not only (13) (I / be fired), but (14) (they / keep) all my computer records, too.

sections.title: E: Inversion transformations
sections.instructions: Complete the sentences so that the meaning remains the same.

items.sentence: Photographs are not permitted under any circumstances.
items.answer: Under no circumstances are photographs permitted.

items.sentence: We've never faced such a serious problem as this.
items.answer: Never before have we faced such a serious problem as this.

items.sentence: I only realised it was Vanessa when she removed her hat.
items.answer: Only when Vanessa removed her hat did I realise it was her.

items.sentence: The audience didn't laugh at any point during the show.
items.answer: At no point during the show did the audience laugh.

items.sentence: As soon as Patrick had finished the e-mail, he sent it.
items.answer: No sooner had Patrick finished the e-mail than he sent it.

items.sentence: It wasn't clear who was going to win the match until the last few minutes.
items.answer: Not until the last few minutes was it clear who was going to win the match.

items.sentence: Dennis passed his driving test and had an accident almost immediately afterwards.
items.answer: Hardly had Dennis passed his driving test when he had an accident.

items.sentence: I didn't learn the result of the match until I read the evening paper.
items.answer: Only when I read the evening paper did I learn the result of the match.

items.sentence: The president will not resign under any circumstances.
items.answer: Under no circumstances will the president resign.

items.sentence: The accused showed no emotion at any time during the trial.
items.answer: At no time during the trial did the accused show any emotion.

sections.title: F: Short answers (So/Neither/Nor)
sections.instructions: Complete the responses to these statements.

items.sentence: 'I wish I had more money.' 'So ______.'
'We never seem to spend much time together in my family.' 'Neither ______.'
'I'm not going to Jacob's party next Sunday.' 'Nor ______.'
'I didn't do my homework last night.' 'Nor ______.'
'I'll be on holiday this time next week.' 'So ______.'
'I had no idea Chris was getting married.' 'Neither ______.'
'I should really get more exercise.' 'So ______.'

sections.title: G: Inversions with as/so/such
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: Such a long way ______ it that we decided to go by train.
So badly ______ I play chess that I almost never agree to have a game.
Pupils are expected to behave politely, as ______ members of staff.
So tired ______ I that I decided to lie down for a while.
Such a threat to society ______ you that I have no choice but to send you to prison.
So cold ______ it last winter that all our pipes froze.
Calculators are banned from the examining room, as ______ dictionaries and handheld computers.
I have been having very strange dreams lately, as ______ my twin sister.

sections.title: H: Form possessives
sections.instructions: Form possessives from the nouns given.

items.sentence: (Billy): ______ opinion
(my mum): ______ cooking
(our next door neighbours): ______ cat
(George the Fifth): ______ daughter
(people): ______ prejudices
(the Greenes): ______ holiday home
(women): ______ magazines
(the boss): ______ car
(Jack and Jill): ______ wedding
(students): ______ marks
(children): ______ shoes
(politicians): ______ promises

sections.title: I: Possessive determiners and pronouns
sections.instructions: Complete using the words in the box. Use each word only once.

items.sentence: Tell Michelle that it's my problem, not ______.
Are you sure this isn't ______ because it looks just like the one I was given for Christmas?
Thanks for the offer, but I think I'll use ______ own money.
I saw a wallet on the floor next to a couple of tourists and asked them if it was ______.
Remind your sister to bring ______ book to the lesson tomorrow.
I'm not sure, but I think ______ Mark's bag, isn't it?
Could you make sure that your children don't leave ______ toys in my garden again, please?
What secrets do you think the Moon might be hiding within ______ craters?

sections.title: J: Error correction (extra words)
sections.instructions: If a line is correct, put a tick (✓). If there is an extra word in a line, write it next to the number.

items.sentence: Well, Trisha, that's my own first day at my new job out of the way.
I wasn't sure what to expect, but it turned out okay.
My manager was really nice, much nicer than yours boss sounds from your letter.
No sooner had I arrived at work than did he made me a cup of tea!
It was a really pleasant surprise.
Not only did he do that, but he was also kind enough to explain to me how everything worked.
The office is really modern and its comfortable.
The other people all have their own desks, but mine desk still hasn't been delivered, so I am sharing with someone else for now.
Little did I not realise when I took the job that I was going to enjoy it so much.
I have finally found a job that I am happy in, and so will you have, I'm sure.
